finding.reachability: Any caller that passes a user-controlled IPv6 string to the exported default function — including SSRF-protection middleware relying on this library to block private IPs.
finding.justification: Line 61 calls ipv6_check(ip) with the raw, un-normalized input string; the regexes on lines 43-50 are case-sensitive (no /i flag), so inputs such as ::FFFF:127.0.0.1, 2001:DB8::1, or 64:FF9B::127.0.0.1 are validated as legitimate IPv6 by ipaddr.js but pass through ipv6_check undetected, causing the function to return false/undefined instead of true.

detection_pattern.section_name: SSRF filter bypass: un-normalized IPv6 passed to case-sensitive regex
detection_pattern.description: Detects places where an IPv6 string is routed to a hand-written case-sensitive regex check using the raw (un-normalized) input rather than a normalized lowercase form, enabling private-range bypass via uppercase characters.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed real. The exported default function (line 61) passes the raw, un-normalized IPv6 string directly to ipv6_check(ip) rather than passing ip.toNormalizedString() or ip.toLowerCase(). ipaddr.js (is_valid / parse) fully accepts mixed-case IPv6 strings such as ::FFFF:127.0.0.1, 2001:DB8::1, and 64:FF9B::127.0.0.1 as valid IPv6 (confirmed by live testing: is_valid returns true, kind() returns 'ipv6', and toNormalizedString() correctly normalizes them to all-lowercase). However, the regexes on lines 43 (/^::f{4}:.../ -- no /i flag), 45 (/^64:ff9b::.../ -- no /i flag), and 49 (/^2001:db8:.../ -- no /i flag, hardcodes lowercase literal 'db8') are case-sensitive and only match lowercase representations. As a result, inputs like ::FFFF:127.0.0.1 pass is_valid(), enter the parsed.kind()==='ipv6' branch, call ipv6_check with the raw uppercase string, and all 13 regexes return false -- so the function returns false (not private) instead of true. Identical addresses in lowercase are correctly blocked. The bypass is unconditional: no non-default options are required, no schema validation guards the input, and the library is documented and positioned for user-controlled IP filtering/SSRF protection. The regexes on lines 51-53 (fc00::/7, fe80::/10, ff00::/8) do carry /i flags but lines 43-50 do not, introducing an inconsistent and exploitable gap for IPv4-mapped (::ffff:), NAT64 (64:ff9b::), and documentation-prefix (2001:db8::) ranges with any uppercase hex.

reachability.reasoning: The vulnerable code path is directly reachable with zero configuration. The exported default function (line 55: export default (ip) => {...}) is the library's primary public API — any consumer can call it directly. No non-default options are required; the function accepts only a single ip string and the ipv6_check(ip) call at line 61 is reached unconditionally whenever ipaddr.js parses the input as IPv6. ipaddr.js accepts mixed-case IPv6 strings (e.g., ::FFFF:127.0.0.1) as valid and returns kind() === 'ipv6', but the raw un-normalized string is passed to ipv6_check() without any case normalization. By contrast, the IPv4 path (line 59) correctly uses parsed.toNormalizedString() before calling ipv4_check() — this inconsistency is the root of the bypass. The regexes on lines 43 (::ffff: mapped), 45 (64:ff9b:: NAT64), and 49 (2001:db8:: documentation) have no /i flag and only match lowercase; uppercase variants such as ::FFFF:127.0.0.1, 64:FF9B::192.168.1.1, and 2001:DB8::1 pass through all 13 regexes undetected and the function returns false (not private) instead of true. The parameter is user-controlled in the library's primary real-world use case: SSRF-protection middleware that passes attacker-supplied IP/hostname strings to this function to decide whether to block the request. No schema validation, allowlist, or type-coercion guards the input before the sink. The bypass is unconditional at default configuration.

finding.reachability: Any caller that passes a user-controlled IPv6 string to the exported default function — including SSRF-protection middleware relying on this library to block private IPs.
finding.justification: Line 61 calls ipv6_check(ip) with the raw, un-normalized input string; the regexes on lines 43-50 are case-sensitive (no /i flag), so inputs such as ::FFFF:127.0.0.1, 2001:DB8::1, or 64:FF9B::127.0.0.1 are validated as legitimate IPv6 by ipaddr.js but pass through ipv6_check undetected, causing the function to return false/undefined instead of true.

detection_pattern.section_name: SSRF filter bypass: un-normalized IPv6 passed to case-sensitive regex
detection_pattern.description: Detects places where an IPv6 string is routed to a hand-written case-sensitive regex check using the raw (un-normalized) input rather than a normalized lowercase form, enabling private-range bypass via uppercase characters.

However, the regexes on lines 43 (/^::f{4}:.../ -- no /i flag), 45 (/^64:ff9b::.../ -- no /i flag), and 49 (/^2001:db8:.../ -- no /i flag, hardcodes lowercase literal 'db8') are case-sensitive and only match lowercase representations. As a result, inputs like ::FFFF:127.0.0.1 pass is_valid(), enter the parsed.kind()==='ipv6' branch, call ipv6_check with the raw uppercase string, and all 13 regexes return false -- so the function returns false (not private) instead of true. The regexes on lines 51-53 (fc00::/7, fe80::/10, ff00::/8) do carry /i flags but lines 43-50 do not, introducing an inconsistent and exploitable gap for IPv4-mapped (::ffff:), NAT64 (64:ff9b::), and documentation-prefix (2001:db8::) ranges with any uppercase hex.

finding.reachability: Any caller that treats a falsy return (undefined) as 'not private' — the most common defensive pattern.
finding.justification: When ipaddr.js validates the input as IPv6 but none of the handwritten regexes match (e.g. due to the case-bypass above), the function falls through ipv6_check() returning false, and then the outer function itself returns undefined — callers using if (!privateIp(addr)) allow() silently permit the request.

detection_pattern.section_name: Insecure design: undefined as 'not private' sentinel
detection_pattern.description: Flags IP-classification helpers that return undefined rather than an explicit boolean, which causes callers relying on truthiness/falsiness to silently pass unknown or malformed addresses as non-private.

verdict.reasoning: The finding's causal chain is factually incorrect. It claims: 'the function falls through ipv6_check() returning false, and then the outer function itself returns undefined.' This is wrong. When is_valid(ip) returns true and parsed.kind() === 'ipv6', the outer function executes 'return ipv6_check(ip)' (line 61), which directly returns whatever ipv6_check returns — boolean false for unmatched IPs, not undefined. The 'return undefined' on line 65 is only reached when is_valid(ip) is false AND the isIP()/ip_regex.v6() fallback also fails, i.e., for entirely invalid/garbage inputs like 'garbage', null, or empty string. The case-bypass bug (uppercase ::FFFF: matching ipv4-mapped private IPs but failing the case-sensitive regex /^::f{4}:.../) is a real separate bug in a different finding class, but it returns boolean false, not undefined. Verified by running the actual module: privateIp('::FFFF:192.168.1.1') returns false (type: boolean); privateIp('garbage') returns undefined (type: undefined). The undefined-return path is not reachable via the claimed vector (valid IPv6 that bypasses the regex).
